hero.title: I translate business goals into scalable products and robust systems
hero.subtitle: I bring clarity to complex products and systems, at the intersection of product, architecture, and engineering.

problems.headline: Does this sound familiar?
problems.subheadline: Typical challenges I keep observing across software projects.
problems.section_resolution: This is where I come in

items.title: Backlog chaos
items.description: Tickets without context. Stories without acceptance criteria. No clear prioritization. The team is busy — but moving in the wrong direction.
consequences: Developers estimate poorly — because context is missing
Features take three times longer than planned
The team is active, but working on the wrong things
items.resolution: As a Product Owner, I bring structure to requirements and prioritization. I create a shared understanding between business and engineering — so everyone knows what comes next and why.
outcomes: Understandable user stories with acceptance criteria

items.title: Design-Dev gap
items.description: Figma designs land with developers who lack technical context. Endless back-and-forth, rework, and frustrated teams on both sides.
items.resolution: I translate design intent into technical specifications. As someone who knows both sides, I spot early where designs create technical problems — before they become sprint blockers.
outcomes: Edge cases and error states documented
Smooth handoff between design and engineering

items.title: Technical debt
items.description: Quick fixes from yesterday block today's features. Nobody knows why decisions were made the way they were.
consequences: Nobody dares to touch core areas anymore
Velocity drops — at the same effort level
items.resolution: As a Solution Architect, I develop strategies to reduce technical debt incrementally — without putting ongoing operations at risk. I separate what needs to be delivered today from what needs to be stabilized long-term.
outcomes: Clear migration paths with defined steps
Maintainable codebase with shorter ramp-up time

items.title: Misalignment
items.description: Business wants roadmaps, engineering wants specs. Stakeholders pursue different goals. In between: nobody translating.
consequences: Requirements keep changing at the last minute
Engineering builds what nobody actually wanted
Trust between business and engineering erodes
items.resolution: I serve as a common language between business and engineering. Through structured workshops, clear decision formats, and transparent communication, I make sure everyone pulls in the same direction.
outcomes: Shared product understanding across the team

problems.resolution_label: This is where I come in
problems.resolution_headline: I bring structure, clarity, and momentum, right where it stalls.
problems.resolution_body: As a seasoned Product Owner, Software Architect, and Full-Stack Engineer, I know both sides: the business pressure and the technical reality. I bridge the gaps, create orientation, and get teams moving in the right direction again.

User Story Mapping.text:
I use User Story Mapping early, as soon as rough requirements are known, but before sprint planning or implementation begins. In a workshop with stakeholders from the business, engineering, and UX, we build a shared view of the actual user process together.

From an often unsorted collection of requirements, three concrete artifacts emerge: a structured backlog hierarchy, a clearly scoped MVP or release plan, and a shared understanding of the user process across the team.

The real outcome is transparency: the team understands the end-to-end flow, dependencies become visible, and prioritization decisions, especially in technical projects, become traceable rather than intuitive.

Stakeholder Management.text:
Technical challenges can usually be solved, but misalignment between stakeholders is often much harder to fix. That's why stakeholder management is one of the most critical success factors in any project for me.

My stakeholder circle ranges from C-level and engineering teams to UX, customers, and external partners. A large part of my work consists of bridging business requirements and technical constraints, acting as a translator between both sides.

When interests conflict, I first try to understand the actual goals behind the demands. That shifts discussions from personal opinions to fact-based decisions, supported by tools like Power/Interest Mapping for prioritization.

The most common root cause: missing transparency about the impact of decisions. Once all stakeholders share the same view of goals, priorities, and consequences, many conflicts resolve on their own.

System Design.text:
System design for me means balancing business requirements, scalability, maintainability, and implementation cost. My focus is on high-level architecture: component structure, service boundaries, data flows, third-party integrations, and technical risks.

The majority of my projects take place in existing system landscapes. The real challenge is rarely designing on a greenfield. It's integrating new requirements into grown architectures while accounting for technical debt, migration paths, and dependencies.

Depending on the phase, different artifacts emerge: architecture diagrams (C4), context and data flow diagrams, ADRs, or management presentations. But the most important outcome isn't the document itself. It's a shared understanding of why a decision was made and what consequences it carries.

API Design (REST/GraphQL).text:
REST or GraphQL. I don't decide ideologically, but based on usage patterns, team structure, and data access complexity. REST when clear resource-based domains and CRUD-heavy use cases dominate. GraphQL as a Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) layer when very different clients need very different data, or when over-/underfetching becomes a real problem.

My core principles regardless of style: clear contracts, consistent error handling, backward compatibility for as long as possible, and no domain chaos from "God APIs".

The most common and costly mistake: APIs modeled from an implementation perspective instead of a domain perspective. Endpoints that mirror tables or internal services 1:1 lead to tight coupling, frequent breaking changes, and workarounds on the client side.

Good API design speaks the domain language of the system, not its internal technical structure.

Cloud Architecture (AWS/GCP).text:
My focus isn't on infrastructure details but on sound architectural decisions at the intersection of product requirements and technical implementation. Core principle: as much managed and serverless as possible, as little infrastructure management as necessary.

I use IaC (Terraform, CDK) where infrastructure needs to be reproducible and versioned, but as the implementation form of a previously defined architectural decision. Concept first, automation second.

Cost vs. scalability is not a technical problem for me. It's a product and growth problem. The starting point is always simplicity: no over-engineered architecture for hypothetical load. Scaling is triggered by metrics, not assumptions. I treat cost as an active architecture parameter. Total Cost of Ownership, not just the cloud bill.

The goal is an architecture that grows with the product, not against it.

Domain-Driven Design.text:
I don't apply DDD dogmatically. I use it contextually. I almost always start strategically: defining Bounded Contexts clearly, separating domain logic from technical details, clarifying ownership. Tactical DDD, meaning Entities, Aggregates, and Repositories, only comes in when the domain is genuinely complex enough to warrant it.

DDD pays off in complex business domains, with multiple parallel teams, and high rates of change in the domain logic. For simple CRUD applications, MVPs, or small teams, it usually just adds overhead.

In teams, I never introduce DDD through theory. I start from real pain points: Why is feature X so hard to change? Why do changes constantly break other parts? Bounded Contexts and Ubiquitous Language become tangible only once the problems are visible.

DDD is a tool for better communication and clearer boundaries, not an architectural religion.

Scrum / Kanban.text:
My choice between Scrum and Kanban depends on context: team maturity, product type, and how plannable the work is. Scrum when something is being actively built and clear iterations make sense. Kanban when continuous flow matters more than sprints, for support, maintenance, or highly variable requirements. In practice, many good setups are hybrids anyway.

My role is not that of a process enforcer, but an adapter: I facilitate the introduction, regularly question whether the setup is still helping, and improve it together with the team, not against it.

The most common failure is Cargo-Cult Agile: Scrum implemented formally, without real agile thinking behind it. Sprints filled mechanically, dailies turned into status reports. With Kanban, often the opposite: no WIP limits, too many parallel tasks.

The framework is just a tool. What matters is whether it helps make better decisions faster and more transparently.

Technical Documentation.text:
I don't document everything equally. I deliberately distinguish between what gives long-term orientation and what's already visible in the code or tests. My focus is on architecture decisions, system flows, dependencies, and technical concepts for complex features.

I write for two groups with different needs, and I consistently separate them into two levels: the Executive View answers why, what the impact is, and what the trade-offs are. The Technical View describes how, what assumptions apply, and what the edge cases are.

On tooling, I'm pragmatic: Markdown in the repo for technical concepts, ADRs for long-term decisions, Confluence for cross-team topics, C4 diagrams for architectural understanding.

Documentation is not an end in itself. It's a tool for reducing misunderstandings and technical debt in communication.

Code Reviews.text:
In code reviews, I don't focus on style. I focus on impact. My priority: does the code actually solve what was functionally required? Does the change fit the existing system design? Are there unintended side effects? Are critical paths covered sensibly, not necessarily a lot, but deliberately?

When I'm not the primary developer, I deliberately avoid co-programming and instead act as a bridge between business logic and technical implementation: Is the functional interpretation correctly implemented? Are there missing edge cases from a business perspective? Is the solution over- or under-engineered?

My role in reviews is often that of a quality and context filter: I don't need to improve every detail, but I make sure no false assumptions get built into the system that will become expensive later.

Figma.text:
I use Figma not as a classic UI/UX designer, but as a collaborative tool between design and engineering. My focus: reviewing designs from a technical perspective, covering feasibility, edge cases, missing states (loading, error, empty), consistency with design systems, and impact on the API and data model.

Figma is for me the single source of truth for UI intent. My role is to make designs engineering-ready: I spot early when a design creates technical complexity, think in component architectures rather than just screens, and translate UI decisions directly into system and implementation decisions.

I also use Claude as a design partner, for quick UI prototyping, generating component drafts, or iterating on layouts without designer dependency. Especially valuable in early MVP phases or for internal tools.

The critical point is the translation layer between design and engineering. When that works cleanly, there's less friction in handoff and less rework in the sprint.

Jira / Linear.text:
Jira or Linear. The choice depends on context. Jira in classic enterprise environments where it's established and integrates well with existing processes. Linear for smaller, product-focused teams or startups where less overhead and faster handling matter.

My focus in backlog management is on structure, clarity, and decision-making capability, not on maximizing ticket count. Clear hierarchy (Epics → Features → Stories → Tasks), consistent prioritization, outcome-focused formulation of items.

One problem I actively avoid is the Zombie Backlog, meaning old, never-prioritized tickets that only create noise. They get either concretized or consistently removed.

The goal is simple: the backlog should always be able to answer, what is the most important problem we should solve next?

TypeScript / React.text:
I'm close to implementation. I regularly write production code in React and React Native projects, not just at the review or concept level. Feature implementation in existing codebases, frontend architecture (state, API layer, folder structure), refactoring larger components.

I appreciate TypeScript not as type safety for its own sake, but as a communication and stability tool for the team: early error detection, self-documenting code that replaces documentation, refactoring safety in larger codebases, faster onboarding for new developers.

My typical context: React Native (Expo-based), React/Next.js for web and dashboards, combined with structured state approaches and clean API layers. What matters to me is that React is cleanly embedded in an overall architecture, covering API, state, and domain structure. In larger apps, that quickly becomes the deciding factor for maintainability.

diagram_captions.user-story-map: Fig. 1 – User Story Map: Activities and user stories in sequential order, sliced by release scope and MVP boundary.
diagram_captions.stakeholder-hub: Fig. 2 – Stakeholder Hub: Relationship network between the central point of contact and all relevant interest groups in the project.
diagram_captions.c4-context: Fig. 3 – C4 Context Diagram: System boundaries, external actors, and their interactions at the highest level of abstraction.
diagram_captions.api-matrix: Fig. 4 – API Design Matrix: Comparison of REST and GraphQL approaches by use case, data access pattern, and interface complexity.
diagram_captions.cloud-evolution: Fig. 5 – Cloud Evolution: Stage model from on-premise monolith through container infrastructure to fully serverless cloud architecture.
diagram_captions.context-map: Fig. 6 – DDD Context Map: Bounded contexts, their strategic relationships, and integration patterns at a glance.
diagram_captions.scrum-kanban-matrix: Fig. 7 – Scrum/Kanban Comparison: Decision matrix for method selection based on predictability, team maturity, and change frequency.
diagram_captions.doc-layers: Fig. 8 – Documentation Layers: Two-tier model of Executive View (Why/Trade-offs) and Technical View (How/Edge Cases).
diagram_captions.review-pyramid: Fig. 9 – Review Pyramid: Prioritization of code review criteria by business correctness, system design conformance, and coverage of critical paths.
diagram_captions.figma-pipeline: Fig. 10 – Figma Pipeline: Handoff process from design intent through technical review to a development-ready component.
diagram_captions.backlog-hierarchy: Fig. 11 – Backlog Hierarchy: Structured levels from Epic through Feature and Story to Task, with prioritization and outcome focus.
diagram_captions.frontend-layers: Fig. 12 – Frontend Architecture Layers: Separation of UI components, state management, API layer, and domain structure in a React environment.

glossary.C4 diagram: Architectural notation with 4 abstraction levels: Context, Container, Component, Code, from bird's eye view to implementation detail.
glossary.C4: Architectural notation with 4 abstraction levels: Context, Container, Component, Code, from bird's eye view to implementation detail.
glossary.ADRs: Architecture Decision Records. They document architectural decisions with context, considered options, and rationale. More durable than any inline comment.
glossary.ADR: Architecture Decision Record. It documents an architectural decision with context, options considered, and rationale.
glossary.Bounded Contexts: DDD concept: clearly bounded areas of a system, each with its own language and ownership. Prevents the same word meaning different things to different teams.
glossary.Ubiquitous Language: A shared language between business and engineering, used consistently in code, docs, and conversation. Core concept from Domain-Driven Design.
glossary.BFF: Backend-for-Frontend. An API layer tailored to one specific client (e.g. web or mobile) that delivers exactly the data that client needs.
glossary.IaC: Infrastructure as Code. Infrastructure is defined as versioned code and provisioned automatically, instead of being configured manually.
glossary.WIP limits: Work-in-Progress limits. They cap the number of parallel tasks in Kanban to enforce focus and make bottlenecks visible.
glossary.MVP: Minimum Viable Product. The smallest version of a product that delivers real user feedback, without building unnecessary features.
glossary.CRUD: Create, Read, Update, Delete. The four basic data operations. CRUD-heavy means systems whose main purpose is managing data records.
glossary.Over-/Underfetching: Over-fetching: API returns more data than needed. Under-fetching: too little, requiring multiple requests. Both waste performance and bandwidth.
glossary.Power/Interest Mapping: Stakeholder analysis method: maps participants by influence (power) and stake (interest) to prioritize communication.
glossary.Cargo-Cult Agile: Agile methods applied formally (sprints, dailies, retros) without the underlying thinking, form without substance.
glossary.Zombie Backlog: A pile of old, never-prioritized tickets that only generate noise. Nobody deletes them, nobody works on them, they just exist.
glossary.Total Cost of Ownership: Total cost of a system over its lifetime: development, operations, maintenance, migration, not just the monthly cloud bill.
glossary.Greenfield: Architecture for a completely new system with no existing constraints, as opposed to Brownfield (evolving existing systems).
glossary.Serverless: Cloud execution model where no server is managed manually. The cloud scales automatically to zero when there's no traffic.

contact.headline: Let's talk.
contact.subheadline:
If you're looking for someone who connects product, architecture, and engineering, equally fluent with business stakeholders and development teams, you're in the right place.

What drives me is building systems that actually work: well-considered, future-proof, with AI as a natural part of the craft. Complex challenges are exactly where I do my best work.
contact.placeholder_message: We're looking for someone in product / architecture and would love to learn more about you...
contact.success: Message transmitted. I'll get back to you within 24h.
contact.error: Transmission error. Please contact me directly by email.

about_me.headline: How I work
about_me.intro:
I work at the intersection of product, architecture, and engineering.

My focus is translating complex requirements into clear system structures – in environments where speed, legacy systems, and multiple stakeholders collide.

I bring scope clarity, technical foresight, and the ability to act as a shared language between business and engineering.

ScopeControl.title: Scope Control & Delivery Focus
ScopeControl.description: I help teams reduce ambiguity in requirements and prevent uncontrolled scope growth. By structuring backlogs and aligning stakeholders early, I ensure that teams always know what actually matters for delivery.

ProductAlignment.title: Product & Technical Alignment
ProductAlignment.description: I translate between product intent and technical implementation. This ensures that what is designed, discussed, and built stays consistent across UX, product, and engineering.

ScalableArchitecture.title: Scalable Frontend Architecture
ScalableArchitecture.description: I design frontend structures that remain maintainable as complexity grows. The goal is not just to ship features, but to keep systems understandable and adaptable over time.

UXInteraction.title: UX Implementation & Interaction Engineering
UXInteraction.description: I bridge the gap between design intent and technical reality. This includes implementing complex UI behavior, interaction patterns, and ensuring that designs remain feasible in production systems.

LegacyIntegration.title: Legacy System Integration
LegacyIntegration.description: I integrate modern frontend systems with existing legacy infrastructure. The focus is on reducing technical friction while enabling gradual modernization instead of disruptive rewrites.

PerformanceScaling.title: Performance & Scalability Engineering
PerformanceScaling.description: I focus on frontend performance patterns that hold up under real-world load. This includes optimizing rendering behavior, data flow, and architectural decisions that impact scalability.

SystemThinking.title: System Thinking in Product Development
SystemThinking.description: I approach frontend development as part of a larger system, not isolated UI work. Every decision is evaluated in terms of its impact on product evolution, team scalability, and long-term maintainability.

StakeholderAlignment.title: Stakeholder Alignment
StakeholderAlignment.description: I align stakeholders across business, design, and engineering teams. By creating transparency around technical decisions, I help teams identify risks early and resolve conflicting priorities more effectively.

ProductOrientedEngineering.title: Product-Oriented Engineering
ProductOrientedEngineering.description: I approach technical decisions through the lens of product success. Rather than simply implementing requirements, I focus on solutions that balance user needs, business goals, and long-term maintainability.

footer.role: Software Engineer, Technical Product Owner & Solution Architect

As a service provider, I am responsible for my own content on these pages in accordance with general laws pursuant to § 7 Para. 1 TMG.

datenschutz.title: Privacy Policy
datenschutz.erhebung_text: Data processing on this website is carried out by the website operator. The contact details of the website operator can be found in the legal notice. No cookies are set and no tracking technologies are used when visiting this website.
datenschutz.kontaktformular_text: If you send me requests via the contact form, your details from the inquiry form, including the contact details you provided, will be stored by me for the purpose of processing the request and in the event of follow-up questions. This data will not be passed on without your consent.
datenschutz.hosting_text: This website is hosted by Vercel. The hoster collects the following data in so-called log files transmitted by your browser: IP address, address of the previously visited website, time of the request, time zone difference to Greenwich Mean Time, content of the request, HTTP status code, amount of data transferred, website from which the request comes and information about the browser and operating system.
datenschutz.rechte_text: You have the right to receive free information about the origin, recipient and purpose of your stored personal data at any time. You also have the right to request the correction or deletion of this data. If you have given consent to data processing, you can revoke this consent at any time for the future. You can contact us at any time for this and other questions on the subject of data protection.
